evidence: "I feel like M is nearby," Jack whispered.
"I'm going to bring the medallion back soon," Jack said loudly. "And the bookmark, too. Both of them. Tomorrow!"
questions.rationale: Jack senses M is near, so he speaks loudly on purpose so that M might hear his promise.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme of this final chapter?

options.A: Solving one mystery can leave an even bigger mystery to explore.
options.B: Friends should always keep their adventures a secret.
options.C: It is safer to stay home than to explore new places.
options.D: Hard work is always rewarded with a good lunch.

evidence: This absolutely proved the tree house belonged to M. There was no doubt about it.
Will we ever meet M? Jack wondered.
questions.rationale: The clue answers who owns the tree house, yet the unanswered question of who M is keeps the larger mystery open.

questions.stem: What two objects does Jack take out of his drawer to bring to the tree house?

options.A: His raincoat and rain boots
options.B: The gold medallion and the leather bookmark
options.C: His notebook and a flashlight
options.D: A castle book and a dinosaur book

evidence: Jack reached into his drawer. He took out the medallion.
Then he took out the bookmark. It was made of blue leather.
questions.rationale: Jack goes to his drawer and takes out the gold medallion and then the blue leather bookmark to carry to the tree house.

questions.stem: How does the green parrot named Polly cause the children's adventure to begin?

options.A: It flies into the tree house and knocks the open book onto the floor
options.B: It appears right after Annie wishes to be in the picture, and then the tree house starts to spin
options.C: It carries the medallion away, so they chase it into the woods
options.D: It reads the title of the book out loud to Jack and Annie

evidence: Annie pointed to the picture. "I wish we were there instead of here," she said.
The tree house started to spin. Faster and faster!
questions.rationale: The parrot from the picture shows up just after Annie makes her wish, and moments later the wind rises and the tree house spins, carrying them away.

questions.stem: In the sentence "The letter M was engraved on it," what does the word "engraved" most nearly mean?

options.A: Cut or carved into the surface
options.B: Painted with bright colors
options.C: Wrapped in leather
options.D: Glued on as a sticker

evidence: It was gold. The letter M was engraved on it.
questions.rationale: The M is engraved on the gold medallion, meaning it is cut or carved into the metal, which is why it matches the M on the tree-house floor.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about the place shown in the open book Annie finds?

options.A: It is a cold, snowy mountain far from the sea
options.B: It is a warm, sunny place by the ocean with a beach, a palm tree, and a ship
options.C: It is the same rainy Frog Creek woods they just left
options.D: It is a busy city full of tall buildings

evidence: He saw a sunny beach. A big green parrot sitting in a palm tree. And a ship sailing on a blue sea.
Annie pointed to the picture. "I wish we were there instead of here," she said.
questions.rationale: The picture shows a sunny beach, a palm tree, and a ship on a blue sea, so the reader can tell it is a warm place by the ocean—very different from the cold rain outside.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie go to the tree house in the rain, find an open book with a sunny sea, and the tree house spins them away
options.B: Jack and Annie decide it is too wet and stay home instead of visiting the tree house
options.C: Jack and Annie return the castle book and dinosaur book to the M person
options.D: Jack and Annie teach the green parrot Polly how to talk

evidence: But we have to go to the tree house," said Annie.
The tree house started to spin. Faster and faster!
questions.rationale: The chapter follows the two hurrying through the storm to the tree house, discovering the open book that shows a sunny sea, and being whisked away as the tree house spins.

questions.stem: What does Jack notice about the sailing ship's flag when the ship comes closer?

options.A: It is bright green with a palm tree on it
options.B: It is black with a skull and crossbones
options.C: It is white with a large letter M
options.D: It is red and yellow like a rainbow

evidence: The flag was black. With a skull and crossbones.
questions.rationale: As the ship nears, Jack can see its flag, which is black with a skull and crossbones—the sign of pirates.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie act differently once the tree house lands by the sea?

options.A: Both of them refuse to leave the tree house at all
options.B: Annie wants to run into the water right away, while Jack wants to study the book first
options.C: Jack jumps in first, and Annie stays behind to read
options.D: Both of them decide to swim out to the sailing ship

evidence: "Come on, let's follow her! Let's go in the water!" said Annie.
“Wait, we have to study the book first,” said Jack.
questions.rationale: Annie is eager to jump straight into the water, but careful Jack wants to study the book before doing anything, showing how their reactions differ.

questions.stem: In the sentence "He shielded his eyes against the sun," what does "shielded" most nearly mean?

options.A: Covered or protected
options.B: Rubbed and scratched
options.C: Closed and rested
options.D: Washed with water

evidence: He shielded his eyes against the sun. And peered out at the sea.
questions.rationale: Jack shields his eyes against the bright sun, meaning he covers or protects them with his hand so he can peer out at the sea.

questions.stem: Why does a chill go through Jack when he sees the ship's flag?

options.A: He realizes the ship is sailing away from them
options.B: He realizes the ship belongs to pirates and may be dangerous
options.C: He is cold because his clothes are still wet from the rain
options.D: He is worried the ship will crash into the palm trees

evidence: The flag was black. With a skull and crossbones.
"Pirates of the Caribbean," Jack read aloud.
questions.rationale: The skull-and-crossbones flag and the book's title tell Jack the ship is a pirate ship, so the chill shows he senses danger coming toward them.

questions.stem: What lesson does this chapter most suggest through what happens to Jack and Annie?

options.A: A beautiful, fun place can still hide a real danger you do not notice at first
options.B: It is always better to swim than to read a book
options.C: You should never leave home when it is raining
options.D: Parrots make the best guides on an island

evidence: Actually, the water did look wonderful.
As he stared at the ship's flag, a chill went through him.
questions.rationale: The children enjoy the wonderful warm water, but the approaching pirate flag shows that even a lovely place can hide danger they only spot too late.

questions.stem: According to the caption Jack reads, what did pirates do three hundred years ago?

options.A: They built stone forts along the coast.
options.B: They raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.
options.C: They mapped every island in the sea.
options.D: They traded gold with the Spanish navy.

evidence: Three hundred years ago, pirates raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.
questions.rationale: The caption states directly that pirates raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie react differently when Annie first sees the pirates?

options.A: Both of them run for the tree house right away.
options.B: Jack hides in the sand while Annie waves at the pirates.
options.C: Annie draws the flag while Jack watches the boat.
options.D: Annie warns Jack and starts running, but Jack keeps drawing instead of looking up.

evidence: "Jack, some pirates are getting into a rowboat," said Annie.
Jack kept drawing.
questions.rationale: Annie notices the danger and calls out, but Jack is so focused on his drawing that he keeps at it instead of reacting.

questions.stem: Jack saw something "glinting in the sand." What does glinting most likely mean here?

options.A: shining with small flashes of light
options.B: sinking down slowly
options.C: making a loud noise
options.D: moving on its own

evidence: Jack saw something glinting in the sand. He reached for it. It was his glasses.
questions.rationale: Because the object turns out to be his glasses catching the light, "glinting" means shining with small flashes of light.

questions.stem: Why does Jack run back toward the water even though the pirates are coming?

options.A: He wants to fight the pirates by himself.
options.B: He is trying to hide the rowboat from Annie.
options.C: He left the pirate book propped in the sand and does not want to lose it.
options.D: He dropped his glasses near the shore.

evidence: Suddenly Jack saw the pirate book. In all the confusion he had forgotten it. It was still propped in the sand.
"I'll be right back!" Jack called. "I've got to get the book!"
questions.rationale: Jack realizes he forgot the pirate book in the sand, so he runs back to grab it before it is lost.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 3?

options.A: Jack and Annie sail out to meet the pirate ship and make friends with the crew.
options.B: Jack learns about pirates and draws their flag, then he and Annie must flee as three pirates row ashore and charge at them.
options.C: Annie reads about the Jolly Roger while Jack looks for his lost glasses in the sand.
options.D: Jack and Annie find pirate treasure buried under a palm tree on the beach.

evidence: He started drawing the Jolly Roger flag.
Three big pirates splashed onto the sand.
questions.rationale: The chapter moves from Jack studying pirates and drawing their flag to the children fleeing as three pirates land and rush at them.

questions.stem: What does the biggest pirate call himself?

options.A: Pinky
options.B: Stinky
options.C: Cap'n Bones
options.D: Jolly Roger

evidence: "No one escapes Cap'n Bones!" he roared. His breath was terrible.
questions.rationale: The biggest pirate roars that no one escapes Cap'n Bones, naming himself.

questions.stem: What does Annie do that shows she is brave even after Cap'n Bones grabs Jack?

options.A: She charges up to the biggest pirate, calls him a bully, and hits and kicks him.
options.B: She quietly climbs back up into the tree house.
options.C: She offers the pirates the medallion so they will let Jack go.
options.D: She runs down the beach to get help from Pinky and Stinky.

evidence: "Leave him alone, you bully!" she cried.
She hit the pirate with her fist and kicked him.
questions.rationale: Instead of hiding, Annie confronts the huge pirate, insults him, and physically fights back, showing her courage.

questions.stem: The chapter is titled "Vile Booty," and Cap'n Bones keeps yelling about wanting gold, not books. In pirate talk, what does booty mean?

options.A: a pirate's boots
options.B: an old sailing map
options.C: a kind of sea bird
options.D: stolen treasure or valuable loot

evidence: "Aghh, books," growled Cap'n Bones. He spit on the sand. "I want gold, you dogs!"
"Gold! Gold! Gold!" he cried.
questions.rationale: Cap'n Bones treasures gold and scorns books, so "booty" means the stolen treasure or valuable loot pirates hunt for.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about why Cap'n Bones suddenly drops Jack and Annie?

options.A: He decides the children are not worth his time.
options.B: He sees the shiny gold medallion and cares far more about the treasure than about them.
options.C: He hears his ship calling him back to sea.
options.D: He is frightened by the gleeful shriek from the tree house.

evidence: Pinky leaned out the tree house window. He held the medallion. It glimmered in the sunlight.
Cap'n Bones dropped Jack and Annie. He caught the medallion as it fell.
questions.rationale: The moment the gold medallion appears, Cap'n Bones lets the children go to grab it, showing greed for treasure outweighs holding them.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme suggested by Chapter 4?

options.A: Greed for gold can control a person, making treasure matter more to them than anything else.
options.B: Reading books is a waste of a pirate's time.
options.C: It is always safer to give bullies whatever they ask for.
options.D: The biggest and strongest person always wins in the end.

evidence: "I hate books! Keep looking, dogs! Find me something good!"
"Gold! Gold! Gold!" he cried. Cap'n Bones threw back his head and laughed horribly.
questions.rationale: Cap'n Bones ignores everything but gold and forgets the children the instant treasure appears, showing how greed can take over a person.

questions.stem: What does Cap'n Bones pull out of his belt pouch to show Jack and Annie?

options.A: A gold coin from the treasure
options.B: A torn treasure map
options.C: A loaded pistol
options.D: A letter from Captain Kidd

evidence: He reached into a belt pouch and pulled out a torn piece of paper. He waved it at Jack and Annie.
"Is that a treasure map?" asked Jack.
questions.rationale: Bones pulls a torn piece of paper from his pouch, and Jack recognizes it as a treasure map.

questions.stem: Why does Cap'n Bones make Jack read the map instead of reading it himself?

options.A: Bones cannot read, so he needs the children to read it for him.
options.B: Bones wants to test whether the children are telling the truth.
options.C: The writing on the map is in a language only Jack knows.
options.D: Bones is too busy aiming his pistols to hold the map.

evidence: "You know he can't read," said Stinky.
“You read it!” Cap'n Bones shoved the map in Jack's face.
